explanation.title: Controlling Element Layout with Display and Position

codeExample.title: Positioned Notification Badge
codeExample.code:
CodeGuru AI — Navigation (sticky)

Notification Badges

The red badges use position: absolute on a position: relative parent.

📬 3
🔔 12
codeExample.explanation:
- `position: sticky; top: 0` on `nav` — scrolls with the page until it hits the top, then sticks there
- `z-index: 10` on `nav` — keeps the nav above any content that scrolls underneath it
- `position: relative` on `.icon-wrapper` — establishes the positioning context for the badge inside it
- `position: absolute; top: -6px; right: -10px` on `.badge` — anchors the badge to the top-right corner of the wrapper
- `position: fixed; bottom: 24px; right: 24px` on `.fab` — locks the button to the viewport corner regardless of scroll

exercise.title: Create a Card with an Absolute Badge
exercise.instructions:
Build a product card with an absolute-positioned sale badge. Create a div with class 'card' that has position relative, a border, padding 20px, max-width 260px, and border-radius 8px. Inside it add a span with class 'sale-badge' that has position absolute, top 0, right 0, background-color #ef4444, color white, padding 4px 10px, and border-radius 0 8px 0 8px. Also add an h3 product name and a p price inside the card.

Expected: card with a red badge in the top-right corner.
exercise.starterCode:
SALE

Mechanical Keyboard

₹2499 ₹3999
exercise.solutionCode:
SALE

Mechanical Keyboard

₹2499 ₹3999
hints: 💡 Add position: relative to .card so the badge can position itself inside it.
💡 Add position: absolute; top: 0; right: 0; to .sale-badge to anchor it to the top-right of the card.
💡 border-radius: 0 8px 0 8px sets corners individually: top-left, top-right, bottom-right, bottom-left.

questions.question: What must a parent element have for position: absolute to work predictably on a child?
options: position: relative (or absolute, fixed, or sticky)
questions.explanation: An absolutely positioned element is placed relative to its nearest ancestor that has a position other than static. If no such ancestor exists, it positions itself relative to the initial viewport. Adding position: relative to the parent element (without changing its visual position) creates the positioning context the child needs.

questions.question: What is the difference between position: fixed and position: sticky?
options: They are identical — both fix the element to the viewport
Fixed is always at the same viewport position; sticky scrolls normally then sticks at a threshold
Sticky is always at the same viewport position; fixed scrolls normally then sticks
Fixed only works horizontally; sticky only works vertically
questions.explanation: position: fixed removes the element from document flow and fixes it at a specific viewport position regardless of scrolling — it never moves. position: sticky keeps the element in the document flow and scrolls with the page normally until it reaches the threshold set by top/bottom/left/right, at which point it sticks until its parent scrolls out of view.
